metadata.from: Milo Olivier (Pfc, 443 Eng Pipeline Co, Fort Leonard Wood, MO) — a buddy of John Hanifen, not a family member
metadata.location_written_from: Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri (443 Engineer Pipeline Company)
metadata.location_sent_to: Not visible on these pages, but addressed to John Hanifen (nicknamed 'Bob' per letterhead annotation)
metadata.envelope_notes: Letterhead features two red castle/fortress insignia icons typical of the Army Corps of Engineers. The name 'Bob' is written in quotation marks at top left, possibly a nickname annotation. No envelope image provided.
topics: catching up with an old friend
metadata.mood_tone: Casual, friendly, and candid — with underlying frustration about military service and envy toward those who have been discharged; humorous and crude at times
metadata.legibility_notes: Generally legible cursive handwriting; a few words at the bottom of page 1 are partially obscured by a torn or folded edge. Profanity is clearly written.
metadata.historical_context: Written during the Korean War era (October 1951). Milo's unit (443 Engineer Pipeline Company) was originally slated to deploy to France, likely as part of NATO buildup, but the deployment was delayed or redirected. Mentions of discharge and deployment reflect the active military mobilization of the early 1950s.

pages.transcription:
"Bob"
443 Engineer
Pipeline Company
                                        Oct. 30, 1951

Hi John!

    Long time no see. When you comeing [sic] home hot rod? if you stay over there much longer you will probable [sic] forget the old U.S.A. I was home on a delay in route and your dad gave me your address. I was suppose [sic] to go to France with this outfit but they couldn't get enough men to fill it up, so they sent another outfit, I guess we are leaveing [sic] sometime after New years. What are you doing now? still driving a truck. I sure will be glad the day we get out of this fucking ar[my], I get to hate it more ev[er]y day.

                                                                        over

Gene De young [sic] got his discharge a couple of weeks ago, he sure brought back a nice cunt from Long Beach. (lucky fucker) well John I'll close for now and write more later.

                        Your Buddie [sic]

                             Milo,

My address is:

    Pfc  Milo Olivier
    US  550 678 92
    443 Eng Pipeline Co
    Fort Leonard Wood
             Mo.

summary: This is a letter dated October 30, 1951, written by Pfc Milo Olivier of the 443 Engineer Pipeline Company at Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, to his friend John Hanifen. Milo reconnected with John after getting his address from John's father during a delay en route home, and writes to catch up, expressing frustration with army life and noting that his unit's planned deployment to France was postponed. He mentions that their mutual acquaintance Gene De Young recently received his discharge and returned home, and closes with his mailing address. The letter is notable for its candid, crude language reflecting the informal camaraderie of enlisted soldiers during the Korean War era.
